explanation: SET NOCOUNT OFF leaves row-count chatter enabled, increasing client and network noise.
SET XACT_ABORT OFF allows run-time errors inside transactions (used later in this proc) to leave them partially applied.
SELECT * couples callers to every current and future column of Sales.Orders.
SELECT TOP (10) without ORDER BY returns an arbitrary set of rows.
DISTINCT on OrderID (primary key of Sales.Orders) is redundant; values are already unique.
WITH (NOLOCK) on Sales.Orders can return uncommitted, missing, or duplicated rows.
YEAR(o.OrderDate) wraps the indexed OrderDate column and prevents a range seek.
Comparing int column CustomerID to nvarchar parameter @CustomerIdText forces implicit conversion and can inhibit seek usage.
OR-based optional filter (@CustomerID IS NULL OR o.CustomerID = @CustomerID) harms cardinality estimation and seeking.
LIKE N'%urgent%' on Comments starts with a wildcard and prevents an index seek.
NOT IN (SELECT i.CustomerID ...) yields no rows if any subquery row is NULL; use NOT EXISTS instead.
ISNULL(o.CustomerID, 0) = 1 wraps the column and makes the predicate non-sargable.
o.OrderID + 0 = @OrderID applies arithmetic to OrderID, preventing direct index matching.
o.CustomerID <> 1 often scans broad portions of the table.
CustomerID = 1 embeds a business identifier directly in the predicate.
CROSS JOIN between Sales.Orders and Sales.Customers has no join predicate and produces a cartesian product.
SELECT TOP (1) without ORDER BY returns an arbitrary row.
Comma-separated FROM list with join predicate in WHERE obscures intent and risks accidental cartesian joins.
SELECT TOP (1) without ORDER BY returns an arbitrary row.
Correlated COUNT(*) subquery against Sales.OrderLines executes once per outer Orders row.
Correlated EXISTS subquery against Sales.OrderLines could be expressed as a set join.
CASE WHEN o.Comments IS NULL THEN 0 ELSE 1 END is repeated in the select list and ORDER BY.
ORDER BY 2 references select-list position and becomes fragile when the list changes.
OFFSET ... FETCH orders only by OrderDate without a unique tie-breaker, making pagination non-deterministic.
UNION between branches filtering on OrderID = 1 and OrderID = 2 performs needless duplicate elimination.
Correlated COUNT(*) > 0 against Sales.OrderLines scans all matching rows when EXISTS would suffice.
SELECT @OrderID = o.OrderID silently keeps the last value when multiple rows are returned.
@OrderIds table variable gets a fixed cardinality estimate; a temp table would optimize better for non-trivial row counts.
SELECT TOP (5) without ORDER BY returns arbitrary rows.
SELECT ... INTO #ImplicitOrders creates the temp table schema implicitly instead of declaring it explicitly.
SELECT TOP (5) without ORDER BY returns arbitrary rows.
DECLARE CURSOR ... OPEN ... FETCH performs row-by-row processing that could be expressed set-wise.
WHILE loop performs iterative work that could usually be a set-based operation.
Concatenates @CustomerID into a SQL string instead of binding it as a parameter.
User-supplied @CustomerID is concatenated directly into executable SQL, enabling injection.
EXEC (@Sql) runs the concatenated string without parameterization.
Concatenates @OrderID into a SQL string instead of binding it as a parameter.
@OrderID is concatenated directly into executable SQL text.
sp_executesql is invoked with a string that has the value embedded rather than bound as a parameter.
#Scratch is created with CREATE TABLE but never dropped before the procedure ends.
CATCH block only SELECTs ERROR_MESSAGE(); it neither rethrows via THROW nor returns a failure status.
RAISERROR is deprecated for new error paths; use THROW instead.
PRINT output is buffered and unsuitable for structured diagnostics or error reporting.
CustomerID = 1 embeds a business identifier directly in the predicate.
'20160101' is an unlabelled date constant embedded in query logic.
CONVERT(date, '01/02/2016') omits an explicit style and depends on session/language settings.
COALESCE on both sides of the join predicate prevents normal join optimization.
SELECT TOP (1) without ORDER BY returns an arbitrary row.
Sales.Orders is joined to itself on OrderID = OrderID, retrieving only data already in the same row.
SELECT TOP (1) without ORDER BY returns an arbitrary row.
ORDER BY o.Comments (nvarchar(MAX)) sorts by a wide, unselective LOB column.
SUM(ol.Quantity) over the entire Sales.OrderLines fact-like table has no restrictive filter.
HAVING ol.OrderID > 0 is a row-level predicate that should be in WHERE.
ORDER BY on a single-row COUNT(*) aggregate is unnecessary.
MERGE has documented concurrency and correctness edge cases; prefer separate UPDATE/INSERT logic.
UPDATE Sales.Orders SET Comments = Comments writes the same value back, causing needless logging and locking.
DELETE FROM Sales.OrderLines WHERE Quantity = -1 deletes by a non-key business value rather than a stable key.
Quantity = -1 embeds a magic business value directly in the predicate.
WAITFOR DELAY '00:00:01' blocks a worker instead of using asynchronous scheduling.
BEGIN TRANSACTION is issued outside a TRY block, leaving errors unhandled.
SET IDENTITY_INSERT Sales.Orders ON changes session state and couples logic to identity values.
EXEC sp_who invokes a system routine without a schema qualifier.
SET ROWCOUNT 1 changes session behavior and is never restored.
INSERT INTO #DemoCategories VALUES (...) omits an explicit column list.
LIKE '%' + @CustomerName + '%' starts with a wildcard and prevents an index seek on CustomerName.
@CustomerName is concatenated into a dynamic SQL string instead of being parameterized.
User-supplied @CustomerName is concatenated directly into executable SQL, enabling injection.
sp_executesql is invoked with a fully concatenated string and no parameter binding.
EXEC sp_executesql calls the system routine without a schema qualifier.
UPDATE #DemoCustomers SET CustomerCategoryID = 1 has no WHERE clause and updates every row.
CustomerCategoryID = 1 embeds a magic value directly in the UPDATE.
Filtering o.OrderDate in WHERE on a LEFT JOIN to Sales.Orders effectively converts it to an inner join.
'2016-01-01' is an unlabelled date constant embedded in query logic.
DeliveryMethodID = NULL never matches; use IS NULL to test for NULL.
dbo.GetCustomerType(CustomerID) in the SELECT list forces row-by-row UDF evaluation.
CATCH block runs inside an open transaction (started at line 239) but does not issue ROLLBACK before handling the error.
CATCH block only SELECTs ERROR_MESSAGE() without rethrowing or returning a failure status.
WITH (INDEX = 1) hardcodes an index hint that may degrade as schema and data evolve.
LIKE '%A%' starts with a wildcard and prevents an index seek on CustomerName.
Three levels of nested derived tables (t1, t2, t3) should be refactored to CTEs or temp tables for clarity and optimization.
SELECT * from dbo.GetCustomerOrdersMulti couples callers to every column returned by the TVF.
GetCustomerOrdersMulti appears to be a multi-statement TVF; an inline TVF would optimize better.
